title: Oxygen Delivery and Consumption: DO2 and VO2

abridged.content:
Oxygen delivery and consumption - the purpose of haemodynamics

Oxygen delivery (DO₂) is the amount of oxygen delivered to tissues each minute. It depends mainly on cardiac output and arterial oxygen content. The formula is DO₂ = CO × CaO₂ × 10. CaO₂ is (1.34 × Hb × SaO₂) + (0.003 × PaO₂).

Haemoglobin-bound oxygen is the major contributor. Dissolved oxygen from PaO₂ is small. A patient can have SpO₂ 100% and PaO₂ 200 mmHg but still have poor DO₂ if haemoglobin or cardiac output is low. CTICU oxygen delivery is a flow, haemoglobin, and saturation problem, not just an oxygen saturation problem.

Oxygen consumption (VO₂) is oxygen used by tissues each minute. Resting adult VO₂ is roughly 200-250 mL/min. It rises with fever, shivering, pain, agitation, seizures, sepsis, catecholamine excess, and increased work of breathing. Normal oxygen extraction is about 20-30%.

Wake-up and extubation often reveal marginal oxygen delivery because VO₂ rises. A sedated ventilated patient may look stable, then develop falling SvO₂ and rising lactate when shivering, pain, or work of breathing increases demand.

expanded.content:
DO₂ and VO₂ - the oxygen economy

The cardiovascular system exists to maintain cellular oxygen supply. Blood pressure, cardiac output, haemoglobin, oxygen saturation, ventilation, and microcirculation matter because they support oxygen delivery and waste removal. DO₂ is oxygen delivered per minute. VO₂ is oxygen consumed per minute.

In CTICU, oxygen economy can fail despite reassuring isolated numbers. SpO₂ may be normal while cardiac output is low. MAP may be normal while SVR is high and flow is poor. Hb may seem acceptable but demand may be high from shivering, fever, pain, or respiratory distress.

Oxygen delivery formula

DO₂ = CO × CaO₂ × 10. CO is in L/min. CaO₂ is in mL O₂/dL. The multiplier 10 converts dL to L.

CaO₂ = (1.34 × Hb × SaO₂) + (0.003 × PaO₂). Hb is in g/dL, SaO₂ is a decimal, and PaO₂ is in mmHg. The constant 1.34 is the approximate mL of oxygen carried by each gram of fully saturated haemoglobin. The 0.003 term is dissolved oxygen.

Haemoglobin dominates oxygen content

Most oxygen is carried on haemoglobin. If Hb is 10 g/dL, SaO₂ 98%, and PaO₂ 100 mmHg, haemoglobin-bound oxygen is 13.1 mL/dL and dissolved oxygen is only 0.3 mL/dL. CaO₂ is about 13.4 mL/dL.

Increasing PaO₂ from 100 to 200 mmHg adds only about 0.3 mL/dL dissolved oxygen. Increasing cardiac output or haemoglobin usually has a much larger effect on DO₂. This is why high oxygen saturation cannot compensate for severe anaemia or low cardiac output.

Cardiac output as the multiplier

If CaO₂ is 13.4 mL/dL and CO is 5 L/min, DO₂ is about 670 mL/min. If CO falls to 3 L/min, DO₂ falls to about 402 mL/min with the same saturation and haemoglobin. This is a common CTICU trap: oxygenation can look excellent while delivery is failing.

Haemoglobin as oxygen carrying capacity

When Hb falls, CaO₂ falls. If Hb decreases from 100 g/L to 70 g/L, DO₂ can drop markedly even when SaO₂ remains high. The body compensates by increasing CO and oxygen extraction. Post-cardiac surgery patients may not be able to compensate because of stunning, RV failure, arrhythmia, vasoplegia, or myocardial ischaemia.

Transfusion decisions are clinical and protocol-driven, but the physiology is clear: anaemia reduces oxygen content, and either cardiac output or extraction must rise to maintain VO₂. If compensation fails, lactate rises and venous saturation falls.

SaO₂ and PaO₂

SaO₂ is the percentage of haemoglobin binding sites occupied by oxygen. Once SaO₂ is near full saturation, extra PaO₂ contributes little to content. PaO₂ still matters for gas exchange reserve, lung severity, and PVR, but it is not the main oxygen content driver once saturation is high.

In RV-risk patients, avoiding hypoxia is vital because hypoxia raises PVR. In low-output patients, however, improving PaO₂ alone will not fix poor DO₂ if CO and Hb are inadequate.

Oxygen consumption

VO₂ is tissue oxygen use per minute. Resting adult VO₂ is about 200-250 mL/min. It increases with fever, shivering, pain, agitation, seizures, sepsis, catecholamine excess, respiratory distress, mobilisation, and inadequate analgesia. It decreases with hypothermia, deep sedation, paralysis, and reduced metabolic activity.

Post-cardiac surgery patients often shift VO₂ quickly. Rewarming and shivering can markedly raise VO₂. Wake-up increases sympathetic tone. Extubation increases work of breathing. If CO or Hb cannot rise to match demand, oxygen extraction rises, SvO₂ falls, and lactate may increase.

Oxygen extraction

Oxygen extraction ratio is VO₂ / DO₂. Normal extraction is around 20-30%. When DO₂ falls, tissues extract more oxygen and venous saturation falls. This compensation has limits. When extraction is maximised, VO₂ becomes supply-dependent and lactate rises.

Supply dependency

Normally VO₂ is maintained despite moderate DO₂ changes because extraction adjusts. In shock, severe anaemia, hypoxaemia, low CO, or microcirculatory failure, VO₂ may become supply-dependent. That means tissues cannot use the oxygen they need because delivery is inadequate or extraction is impaired. Organ dysfunction and lactic acidosis follow.

Worked examples

Example 1: Hb 100 g/L or 10 g/dL, SaO₂ 98%, PaO₂ 100 mmHg, CO 5 L/min. CaO₂ is about 13.4 mL/dL. DO₂ is 670 mL/min.

Example 2: Same oxygen content but CO falls to 3 L/min. DO₂ falls to about 402 mL/min. SpO₂ may still read 98%.

Example 3: Hb falls to 70 g/L, SaO₂ remains 98%, PaO₂ remains 100 mmHg, and CO remains 5 L/min. CaO₂ becomes about 9.5 mL/dL. DO₂ becomes about 475 mL/min.

Example 4: Hb 80 g/L, SpO₂ 96%, CO 3.2 L/min, fever and shivering. DO₂ is reduced by low Hb and low CO while VO₂ is increased by shivering and fever. This is a high-risk delivery-demand mismatch.

Bedside application

  1. Assess content. Check Hb, SaO₂/SpO₂, PaO₂, and whether oxygenation is adequate for RV and organ needs.

  2. Assess flow. Check CO/CI, pulse pressure, arterial waveform, echo, lactate, urine output, and skin perfusion.

  3. Assess demand. Check fever, shivering, pain, agitation, seizures, respiratory distress, sepsis, and catecholamine load.

  4. Assess extraction. Use SvO₂/ScvO₂, lactate, base excess, and organ function.

  5. Treat the limiting variable. Improve CO, Hb, SaO₂, perfusion pressure, ventilation, analgesia, sedation, temperature, or source control as indicated.

pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls - DO₂ and VO₂

Pearl 1 - SpO₂ is not oxygen delivery

SpO₂ does not measure haemoglobin or cardiac output. A patient can be fully saturated and still have poor DO₂.

Pearl 2 - PaO₂ adds little content once saturation is high

Most oxygen is haemoglobin-bound. Dissolved oxygen is small, so very high PaO₂ cannot compensate well for low Hb or low CO.

Pearl 3 - Wake-up increases demand

Shivering, pain, agitation, and work of breathing increase VO₂ and can expose marginal delivery.

Pearl 4 - Low SvO₂ usually means extraction has increased

Low delivery or high demand makes tissues extract more oxygen, lowering venous saturation.

Pearl 5 - Treat the limiting variable

More oxygen will not fix low DO₂ if the key issue is low cardiac output or anaemia. Sedation, warming, analgesia, ventilation, transfusion, or inotropy may be the more relevant intervention depending on the pattern.

equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In - DO₂ and VO₂

Pulse oximeter

SpO₂ estimates saturation but not Hb or CO. Poor waveform, low perfusion, motion, nail polish, dyshemoglobins, and vasoconstriction can affect accuracy.

ABG analyser

ABG provides PaO₂, pH, PaCO₂, lactate, base excess, and often Hb/co-oximetry. These assess content, ventilation, acid-base status, and metabolic stress.

PAC/haemodynamic monitor

CO/CI is central because it multiplies oxygen content. PAC also gives SvO₂, a delivery-demand marker.

Temperature monitoring

Fever and shivering increase VO₂. Hypothermia reduces metabolism but impairs coagulation and contractility.

Ventilator

Ventilation affects oxygenation, PaCO₂, work of breathing, PVR, venous return, and therefore both DO₂ and VO₂.

targets.content:
Targets and Numbers - DO₂ and VO₂

Resting adult VO₂ is about 200-250 mL/min. Normal DO₂ is often roughly 900-1100 mL/min in a typical adult. Oxygen extraction is about 20-30%. Practical CTICU targets include SpO₂ usually ≥94% unless otherwise prescribed, CI ≥2.2 L/min/m², lactate <2 mmol/L or falling, urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, and SvO₂ usually 60-80% or at least ≥65-70% when used as a target.

tables.heading: Oxygen economy values
rows: Falls with low CO, Hb, or SaO₂
Rises with fever, shivering, pain, work of breathing
Rises when delivery falls or demand rises
Low CI causes poor DO₂ despite normal SpO₂
Rising suggests mismatch or metabolic stress

tables.heading: DO₂ problem-solving
rows: Low CI, low SvO₂, lactate, oliguria
Fix preload, rhythm, contractility, afterload, obstruction

quizQuestions.question: Which three variables mainly determine oxygen delivery?
options: Cardiac output, haemoglobin, and arterial oxygen saturation
Respiratory rate, urine output, and potassium
quizQuestions.explanation: DO₂ depends on CO and arterial oxygen content. Content depends mainly on Hb and SaO₂.

quizQuestions.question: SpO₂ 100%, Hb 70 g/L, CI 1.8, rising lactate, low SvO₂. Best interpretation?
options: Oxygen delivery is likely inadequate despite normal saturation
quizQuestions.explanation: Saturation is normal but content and flow are low, so DO₂ is likely inadequate.

quizQuestions.question: Why can shivering cause haemodynamic deterioration after cardiac surgery?
options: It increases VO₂ so marginal DO₂ may no longer meet demand
quizQuestions.explanation: Shivering markedly raises oxygen consumption. If CO or Hb cannot increase, extraction rises, SvO₂ falls, and lactate may rise.
